column1: Binary Trees,level order traversal,https://practice.geeksforgeeks.org/problems/level-order-traversal/1,"1. Problem Summary: Level order traversal of a binary tree involves visiting all the nodes at each depth level from left to right before moving on to nodes at the next depth level. This is also known as Breadth-First Search (BFS) traversal for trees.

2. Approach: The optimal method to achieve level order traversal is by using a queue. Start by pushing the root node into the queue. While the queue is not empty, remove the front node, record its value, and then push its left and right children (if they exist) into the queue in that order. This ensures that nodes are visited level by level and from left to right within each level. The use of the queue is crucial as it naturally follows the FIFO (First In First Out) principle required for level order traversal.

4. Time Complexity and Space Complexity: The time complexity is O(n), where n is the number of nodes in the tree, since each node is processed exactly once. The space complexity is O(m), where m is the maximum number of nodes at any level in the tree, which is required for the queue."

Binary Trees,Reverse Level Order traversal,https://practice.geeksforgeeks.org/problems/reverse-level-order-traversal/1,"1. Problem Summary: Reverse Level Order traversal of a binary tree involves traversing the tree from the bottom level to the top level, and from left to right within each level. The output is a list of node values in this specific order.

2. Approach: The optimal way to achieve Reverse Level Order traversal is by using a queue for level order traversal and a stack to reverse the order. Start by pushing the root node into the queue. For each node processed, push its children (first right, then left to maintain left-to-right order in final output) into the queue. Simultaneously, push the node's value into a stack. This ensures that the lowermost nodes' values are on top of the stack. After processing all nodes, pop values from the stack to get them in bottom-up order.

4. Time Complexity and Space Complexity: The time complexity is O(N) where N is the number of nodes in the tree, as each node is processed exactly once. The space complexity is also O(N) due to the usage of a queue and a stack, both of which can hold at most N elements in the worst case."

Binary Trees,Height of a tree,https://practice.geeksforgeeks.org/problems/height-of-binary-tree/1,"1. Problem Summary: The height of a binary tree is defined as the maximum depth from the root node to the furthest leaf node. The problem involves determining this height for a given binary tree.

2. Approach: To find the height of a binary tree, we can use a recursive approach. Starting from the root, recursively determine the height of the left and right subtrees. The height of the tree rooted at any node will be one more than the maximum height of its left and right subtrees. This approach leverages the divide-and-conquer strategy by breaking the problem down into smaller subproblems (i.e., heights of subtrees) and combining their results. The recursion naturally follows the tree structure, making it intuitive and easy to remember.

Binary Trees,Diameter of a tree,https://practice.geeksforgeeks.org/problems/diameter-of-binary-tree/1,"1. Problem Summary: The diameter of a binary tree is the length of the longest path between any two nodes in the tree. This path may or may not pass through the root.

For each node, the potential diameter is the sum of the heights of its left and right subtrees. Update the maximum diameter at each node. This approach ensures that each node is visited once and both height and maximum path calculations are done in the same traversal, optimizing the process.

Binary Trees,Mirror of a tree,https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/create-a-mirror-tree-from-the-given-binary-tree/,"1. Problem Summary:

The ""Mirror of a tree"" problem involves creating a mirror image of a binary tree such that the structure of the tree is flipped around its central axis. In simpler terms, all left children become right children and vice versa throughout the entire tree.

To mirror a binary tree, we recursively swap the left and right children of every node in the tree. Start from the root and swap its children, then recursively apply the same procedure to the left child (which after swapping becomes the right child) and the right child (which becomes the left child). This recursive approach ensures that each subtree is mirrored. The process is intuitive as it mimics the physical action of flipping or mirroring an image, where elements on the left move to the right and vice versa.

Binary Trees,Inorder Traversal of a tree both using recursion and Iteration,https://www.techiedelight.com/inorder-tree-traversal-iterative-recursive/,"1. Problem Summary:
Inorder traversal of a binary tree involves visiting the nodes in a left-root-right sequence. This problem can be solved using both recursive and iterative methods.

For the recursive approach, the function calls itself to traverse the left subtree, then processes the current node, and finally calls itself to traverse the right subtree. This is a natural and straightforward method following the definition of inorder traversal. For the iterative approach, a stack is used to simulate the recursion. Start by pushing all left children of the root into the stack until there are no more left children. Then, pop a node from the stack, process it, and if it has a right child, repeat the process starting from this right child. This method effectively mimics the recursive stack behavior using an explicit stack data structure.

Both recursive and iterative approaches have a time complexity of O(n), where n is the number of nodes in the tree. The space complexity is also O(n) in the worst case due to the depth of the recursion stack or the size of the iterative stack, respectively."

Binary Trees,Preorder Traversal of a tree both using recursion and Iteration,https://www.techiedelight.com/preorder-tree-traversal-iterative-recursive/,"1. Problem Summary: Preorder traversal of a binary tree involves visiting the root node first, followed by the recursive traversal of the left subtree, and then the right subtree. The challenge is to implement this traversal both recursively and using an iterative approach.

- Recursive Approach: Begin at the root and process it (e.g., print/store the value). Recursively apply the same procedure first to the left child and then to the right child. This approach directly follows the definition of preorder traversal.

- Iterative Approach: Use a stack to simulate the recursion. Start by pushing the root node onto the stack. While the stack is not empty, pop the top node, process it, and push its right child followed by its left child onto the stack (right child first to ensure the left child is processed first as the stack is LIFO).

4. Time Complexity and Space Complexity: Both recursive and iterative approaches have a time complexity of O(n), where n is the number of nodes in the tree. The space complexity is also O(n) due to the use of the system stack for recursion or an explicit stack for the iterative approach."
Binary Trees,Postorder Traversal of a tree both using recursion and Iteration,https://www.techiedelight.com/postorder-tree-traversal-iterative-recursive/,"1. Problem Summary:

The task is to perform a postorder traversal on a binary tree, where each node is visited in the order of left child, right child, and then the node itself. This traversal should be implemented using both recursive and iterative methods.

For the recursive approach, the function recursively calls itself first on the left child, then on the right child, and finally processes the current node. For the iterative approach, use two stacks. Push the root to the first stack. Pop from the first stack, and push the node to the second stack, then push its left and then right children to the first stack. Continue until the first stack is empty. The second stack will have the nodes in postorder when popped. This method leverages the LIFO property of stacks to reverse the order of processing nodes.

Both recursive and iterative approaches have a time complexity of O(n), where n is the number of nodes in the tree. The space complexity is also O(n) due to the use of system stack in recursion and explicit stacks in the iterative method."
Binary Trees,Left View of a tree,https://practice.geeksforgeeks.org/problems/left-view-of-binary-tree/1,"1. Problem Summary:

The ""Left View of a Tree"" problem involves printing or returning the nodes visible when a binary tree is viewed from the left side. This view captures the first node visible at each level when traversed from top to bottom.

The optimal way to solve this problem is by using a level-order traversal (Breadth-First Search) with the help of a queue. Start by pushing the root node into the queue. For each level, the number of elements in the queue corresponds to the number of nodes at that level. Process each level by dequeuing all nodes of the current level and enqueueing all children of these nodes. The first node dequeued at each level is part of the left view. This approach ensures that you always process the leftmost node of each level first, capturing the left view effectively.

Binary Trees,Zig-Zag traversal of a binary tree,https://practice.geeksforgeeks.org/problems/zigzag-tree-traversal/1,"1. Problem Summary:

The Zig-Zag traversal of a binary tree involves visiting nodes level by level but alternating between left-to-right and right-to-left order for each level. This results in a zig-zag pattern of node visits.

To achieve a zig-zag traversal, use two stacks. The first stack (`currentLevel`) is used for traversing the current level, and the second stack (`nextLevel`) is used to prepare for the next level. Start by pushing the root node into `currentLevel`. For each node processed, push its children to `nextLevel` in an order determined by the current direction of traversal (left-to-right or right-to-left). Once `currentLevel` is empty, swap it with `nextLevel` and reverse the traversal direction. This alternating mechanism between the two stacks and the direction ensures the zig-zag pattern. This approach intuitively mimics a wave-like or zig-zag motion through the use of stack reversal and level-wise processing.

The time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes in the binary tree, as each node is processed exactly once. The space complexity is O(n) in the worst case, due to storage of nodes in the stacks."

Binary Trees,Check if a tree is balanced or not,https://practice.geeksforgeeks.org/problems/check-for-balanced-tree/1,"1. Problem Summary: Determine if a binary tree is balanced. A binary tree is considered balanced if, for every node, the height difference between its left and right subtrees is no more than one.

2. Approach: The optimal algorithm involves a depth-first search (DFS) that computes the height of each subtree while simultaneously checking for balance at each node. By calculating the height from the leaf nodes upwards, we can determine the balance condition at each step without revisiting nodes multiple times. If at any node the height difference between its left and right subtree exceeds one, the function will immediately return an error code (commonly `-1`). This approach avoids unnecessary computations and early exits if the tree is found unbalanced.

Binary Trees,Diagnol Traversal of a Binary tree,https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/diagonal-traversal-of-binary-tree/,"1. Problem Summary: In the ""Diagonal Traversal of a Binary Tree"" problem, we are required to traverse a binary tree diagonally. Each diagonal contains all nodes that have the same difference between their levels and horizontal distances from the root.

2. Approach: The optimal way to solve this problem is by using a breadth-first search (BFS) with a queue. Nodes are pushed into the queue with an associated diagonal number, which is incremented when moving left and kept the same when moving right. This helps in grouping nodes that are on the same diagonal. Start with the root node at diagonal 0. For each node processed, insert its right child into the queue (same diagonal) and its left child (increment diagonal by 1). This ensures all nodes on the same diagonal are processed continuously. Keep track of nodes under each diagonal using a map, and once the traversal is complete, extract the diagonals from the map for the final result.
4. Time Complexity and Space Complexity: The time complexity is O(N) as each node is processed once, and the space complexity is also O(N) to store the nodes' values in the map and queue."
Binary Trees,Boundary traversal of a Binary tree,https://practice.geeksforgeeks.org/problems/boundary-traversal-of-binary-tree/1,"1. Problem Summary:

The Boundary Traversal of a Binary Tree involves traversing and printing the nodes that form the boundary of the tree. This includes the left boundary (excluding the leaf nodes), the leaf nodes, and the right boundary (excluding the leaf nodes, traversed from bottom to top).

To solve this, break the problem into three main parts: traversing the left boundary (excluding leaves), collecting all leaf nodes, and traversing the right boundary in reverse (excluding leaves). Start by identifying and printing the left boundary nodes from the root downwards, stopping before leaf nodes. Then, use a separate function to recursively collect and print all leaf nodes from left to right. Finally, collect right boundary nodes using recursion but print them from bottom to top, which can be achieved by storing them first (e.g., in a stack) before printing.

Binary Trees,Convert Binary tree into Sum tree,https://practice.geeksforgeeks.org/problems/transform-to-sum-tree/1,"1. Problem Summary: The problem requires transforming a given binary tree into a sum tree where each node's value is updated to be the sum of the values of its left and right subtree nodes, not including its original value.

2. Approach: The optimal approach involves a recursive post-order traversal of the tree. In a post-order traversal, we first process the left and right children and then the node itself. This ensures that the subtree values are computed before the node's value is updated. During each recursive call, compute the sum of the node's left and right subtrees, update the node's value to this sum, and return the sum of the node's original value and its updated value to the parent node. This way, each node is effectively transformed based on its descendants before contributing to its parent's value.

To check if two trees are mirror images, we compare their roots and then recursively check the following: the left subtree of the first tree with the right subtree of the second tree, and the right subtree of the first tree with the left subtree of the second tree. Binary Trees,Find Largest subtree sum in a tree,https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/find-largest-subtree-sum-tree/,"1. Problem Summary: The problem involves finding the largest sum of all nodes' values in any subtree of a given binary tree. A subtree includes any node and all of its descendants.

2. Approach: The optimal approach to solve this problem is to use a post-order traversal (left-right-root) of the tree. During the traversal, calculate the sum of nodes for each subtree and simultaneously keep track of the maximum subtree sum encountered. This approach leverages recursion to explore each node and its children, compute the sum of the subtree rooted at that node, and update the maximum sum if the current subtree's sum is greater than the previously recorded maximum.

Binary Trees,Maximum Sum of nodes in Binary tree such that no two are adjacent ,https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/maximum-sum-nodes-binary-tree-no-two-adjacent/,"1. Problem Summary:
The problem requires finding the maximum sum of nodes in a binary tree such that no two adjacent nodes (direct parent-child relationship) are included in the sum.

This problem can be efficiently solved using a post-order traversal with dynamic programming. For each node, calculate two values: the maximum sum including the node (`incl`) and the maximum sum excluding the node (`excl`). For each child, `incl` of the current node will add up `excl` of the child (since including current node means we cannot include its children), and `excl` of the current node will be the max of `incl` or `excl` of its children (giving flexibility to either include or exclude each child). This approach avoids recomputation by storing results for each subtree as it is processed, ensuring each node is only processed once.
int excl = max(left.first, left.second) + max(right.first, right.second)
The time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes in the binary tree, as each node is processed once. The space complexity is O(h) due to recursion stack, where h is the height of the tree."

2. Approach: The optimal approach uses a recursive traversal of the binary tree. Starting from the root, recursively search for the nodes in the left and right subtrees. If one of the nodes matches the current node, return it as a potential LCA. If both left and right subtree calls return non-null values, it indicates that the current node is the LCA, as one node exists in the left subtree and the other in the right. If only one of the subtree calls returns a non-null, propagate this node upwards as a potential ancestor. This approach intuitively follows the path from root to the nodes, marking potential ancestors and confirming the LCA when paths from two nodes intersect.

Problem Summary: The problem requires identifying all subtrees within a given binary tree that appear more than once. A subtree is considered a duplicate if there is another subtree in the binary tree that has the same structure and node values.

2. Approach: The optimal way to solve this problem is by using a depth-first search (DFS) to serialize each subtree as a string and store these strings in a hash map to count their occurrences. During the traversal, each node's subtree is serialized into a string representation (like pre-order traversal) that includes node values and structure. If the serialized form of a subtree is already in the hash map and it's the first time it is repeated, add the root node of this subtree to the result list. This serialization helps in easily comparing the structure and values of different subtrees.

Binary Trees,Tree Isomorphism Problem,https://practice.geeksforgeeks.org/problems/check-if-tree-is-isomorphic/1,"1. Problem Summary: The Tree Isomorphism Problem involves determining if two given binary trees are isomorphic. Two trees are isomorphic if one of them can be transformed into the other by swapping left and right children of some of the nodes.

2. Approach: To solve the Tree Isomorphism Problem, we can use a recursive approach. The base case is when both nodes being compared are null, in which case they are trivially isomorphic. If one node is null and the other is not, they aren't isomorphic. For non-null nodes, check if the data of the nodes are the same. Then, recursively check two conditions: normal children alignment (left with left, right with right) and swapped children alignment (left with right, right with left). The trees are isomorphic if either of these recursive checks return true. This approach intuitively checks all possible alignments by exploring each branch.
bool normal = isIsomorphic(n1->left, n2->left) && isIsomorphic(n1->right, n2->right)
bool swapped = isIsomorphic(n1->left, n2->right) && isIsomorphic(n1->right, n2->left)

4. Time Complexity and Space Complexity: The time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes in the tree, since each node is visited once. The space complexity is O(h) where h is the height of the tree, due to the recursion stack."
